recommended_first_slice.name: configurable_notification_icon_via_platform_bridge
recommended_first_slice.consumer_purpose: Allow an operator or integrator to customize the notification icon URL without rebuilding the desktop app, proving the customization pipeline (config → store → IPC → renderer) end-to-end.
claim_atoms: Notification icon URL is no longer hardcoded in renderer source
Icon URL is configurable via electron-store with an opaque key
A new Platform method exposes the icon override to the app framework
The existing IPC channel pattern (registerIpcHandlers) is used for the store read
Default value preserves current behavior when no override is set
No changes required to @opencode-ai/plugin, PluginLoader, or opencode core
No breaking changes to existing Platform consumers
recommended_first_slice.rationale: B1's 'notification icon URL via Platform' is the narrowest possible probe that proves the customization pipeline works end-to-end. B2's 'adapt TUI slot system for Electron/SolidJS' is architecturally premature: (1) the TUI slot system depends on @opentui/core and @opentui/solid — terminal-specific libraries whose Renderable/SlotMode/CliRenderer types do not map to DOM rendering; (2) PluginLoader depends on Effect's Context/Layer system, which the desktop main process does not use; (3) a new desktop plugin kind would require 10+ files across 4 packages without a single concrete consumer to validate against; (4) two files (ipc.ts, types.ts) already have in-flight migration edits from another session. The notification icon URL is a real hardcoded value (index.tsx:234) with a clean customization path: Platform method → IPC handler → electron-store. Once this pipeline is proven, adding more configurable values follows the same pattern. The desktop plugin system (slot-based UI, hook registration) is the correct long-term architecture but should be deferred to slice 2+ after the pipeline is proven.

alternatives_considered.name: desktop_platform_slot_system
pros: Proves a general extensibility pattern for desktop UI
Reuses PluginLoader infrastructure (resolution, lifecycle, config spec)
Enables third-party desktop plugins without modifying core
cons: TUI slot system depends on @opentui/core/solid — terminal types do not map to Electron DOM
PluginLoader depends on Effect Context/Layer — desktop process has no Effect dependency
Blast radius: 10+ files across plugin, desktop, opencode, and app packages
No existing desktop plugin exists to validate the new plugin kind
Two target files (ipc.ts, types.ts) have in-flight edits from concurrent sessions
Building a slot system without proving the config pipeline first risks solving the wrong abstraction layer
High reversibility cost: once a DesktopPlugin type is in @opencode-ai/plugin, removing or changing it is a breaking change
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: This is the correct long-term architecture but it is too heavy for the first slice. The monorepo needs to prove the simpler 'config value → persistence → renderer' pipeline before building plugin infrastructure on top of it. Every infrastructure element the slot system needs (PluginLoader, Effect layers, npm plugin resolution) can be proven incrementally. The first slice must be falsifiable and reversible within a single session.

alternatives_considered.name: configurable_window_title_via_platform_bridge
pros: High visibility — user sees the window title immediately
Same blast radius as notification icon (Platform + IPC + store)
cons: Window title is set in main process (windows.ts:130), not renderer — requires different IPC direction
Main process window creation is synchronous; async store read requires refactoring createMainWindow
Notification icon is purely optional (Platform method with fallback); window title has no graceful fallback UX
The existing electron-store IPC interface already supports arbitrary keys — proving the store part is not novel
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Window title changes require modifying the BrowserWindow creation path in the main process, which is harder to test and has synchronous timing constraints. The notification icon is purely additive in the renderer, making it easier to verify and roll back.

alternatives_considered.name: external_link_rewriting_platform_method
pros: Addresses white-labeling of deep links (opencode:// → custom://)
cons: Must handle 'opencode://' protocol registration in electron-builder config and main process
Protocol registration is per-platform (macOS Info.plist, Windows registry, Linux .desktop)
Rewriting deep links at runtime is fragile — system-level protocol handlers are set at install time
Harder to test end-to-end than a notification icon
alternatives_considered.why_rejected: Protocol customization is inherently a build-time/install-time concern, not a runtime customization. The first slice should prove the runtime pipeline, not the build configuration pipeline.

risks: Two target files (packages/desktop/src/main/ipc.ts, packages/desktop/src/preload/types.ts) have in-flight migration edits from a concurrent session. Any edit to these files must be additive and must not remove or conflict with the SqliteMigrationProgress→MigrationProgress rename already staged.

The notification icon uses the Notification API in the renderer process — Electron's Notification API may behave differently across platforms. Testing must include macOS, Windows, and Linux notification rendering.
If the Platform method is added as required (non-optional), all Platform implementors must be updated. Must use optional method signature to avoid breaking web platform.
electron-store is available in main process only — the renderer cannot read from store directly, so the IPC round-trip latency must be acceptable for notification display.
The app framework (packages/app) may not render notifications through Platform.notify() in all contexts — should verify the call path from @opencode-ai/app's notification system to Platform.notify.
deferred_seams: Desktop plugin system (DesktopPlugin type in @opencode-ai/plugin, slot-based UI injection for Electron/SolidJS) — deferred to slice 2+ after the config pipeline is proven
Customizable window chrome (title, dimensions, traffic light position, frame style) — deferred; requires main-process BrowserWindow option wiring
Configurable protocol/deep-link scheme — deferred; requires install-time configuration changes and is a build concern
Desktop UI slot injection (equivalent to TUI slots: sidebar_content, home_logo, etc.) — deferred; depends on proving the plugin infrastructure first
Config-driven themes beyond the existing JSON theme system — themes already have a working pipeline via import.meta.glob and CSS variable injection
Custom menu entries and accelerators — deferred; requires extending the declarative DESKTOP_MENU pattern from packages/app
Custom sidecar environment variables — deferred; the sidecar env is assembled in server.ts from a fixed list
plugin section in opencode.json for desktop-specific plugins — deferred; requires a desktop plugin kind and loader support
evidence_plan:
The customization is proven end-to-end by the following evidence chain:
1. Unit test: electron-store entry for 'notificationIconUrl' can be written and read (test against main process store)
2. IPC test: the new IPC channel for reading a custom icon URL returns the expected value or null
3. Platform test: createPlatform() in renderer/index.tsx returns a notify() method that uses the configurable icon URL when set, falling back to the default when not set
4. Integration test: a Notification created with the custom icon URL appears with the expected icon (requires Electron integration test framework, or manual verification on each platform)
5. Regression test: with no override configured, the notification icon is the default opencode.ai URL — current behavior is preserved
6. Falsification test: set a non-existent icon URL — verify notification still appears with fallback behavior (no crash on missing icon)

The key falsifier: if the notification icon URL cannot be overridden at runtime without a rebuild, the claim is falsified.
attack_surface:
An adversary would attempt to falsify this slice by:
1. **Isolation attack**: opencode.json plugin discovery should NOT affect the notification icon — the icon is Platform-configurable, not plugin-configurable. If a plugin can override the icon, the boundary between 'config value' and 'plugin contribution' is broken.
2. **Persistence attack**: corrupt the electron-store JSON file — verify the app handles corrupt store gracefully and falls back to the default URL.
3. **Latency attack**: delay the IPC round-trip — if the store read takes >500ms, notifications should still appear with the default icon (async fallback).
4. **Content security attack**: the icon URL is passed to new Notification(url) — verify no XSS/privilege escalation via javascript: or file: URLs. The URL must be validated as an http(s) URL before passing to the Notification API.
5. **Rollback attack**: after setting a custom icon URL, clear the store — verify automatic fallback to the hardcoded default URL.
6. **Fresh install attack**: on a fresh install with no store entries, verify the default icon URL is used (no KeyError or null-reference on missing store key).

tool_ux.issue: glob pattern in docs/schemas/ returns files but not directories — cannot enumerate subdirectories to check for learning schema files
tool_ux.expected: Ability to use patterns like docs/schemas/* or docs/**/*.json
tool_ux.actual: Glob works but only matches files, not directories; cannot tell if a schemas/ subdirectory exists
tool_ux.workaround: Use bash with fd or ls for directory structure inspection

tool_ux.issue: The bash allowlist shows `git diff*` is allowed but `git diff packages/desktop/src/main/ipc.ts` is rejected with 'denied by a user-defined permission'
tool_ux.expected: Pattern `git diff*` should match `git diff packages/desktop/src/main/ipc.ts`
tool_ux.actual: The command is rejected despite matching the allowed pattern
tool_ux.workaround: None discovered — use the difference between `git status` and read_source to infer dirty file state
